commit.message:
feat: implement mappers 141-149 (Sachen SA-013/72007/0036/0037 family) (#1938)

* feat: implement mappers 141–149 (Sachen SA-013/72007/0036/0037 family)

Mappers 141, 142, 144, 146 are aliases to existing implementations:
- 141 → Sachen8259 Variant A (same hardware as mapper 135)
- 142 → Mapper56 / Kaiser KS202 (same hardware as mapper 56)
- 144 → ColorDreamsMapper (same hardware as mapper 11)
- 146 → Mapper79 / NINA-03/06 (same hardware as mapper 79)

New mapper structs added for the remaining four:
- Mapper143 (SA-013): fixed 16KB×2 PRG + CHR, copy-protection
  read window at $4100–$5FFF returning address-derived value
- Mapper145 (SA-72007): fixed 32KB PRG, 1-bit CHR bank via bit 7
  of writes decoded with (addr & 0x4100) == 0x4100
- Mapper147 (SA-0036 / JV001): 32KB switchable PRG + 8KB CHR driven
  by TxcJv001Chip ASIC (4-bit mask, initial invert=true); value is
  rotated right 2 before passing to chip on write, rotated left 2
  on chip read
- Mapper149 (SA-0037): fixed 32KB PRG, 1-bit CHR bank via bit 7 of
  writes to $8000–$FFFF

All eight mappers registered in the factory and added to
SUPPORTED_MAPPERS. 37 new unit tests added.

Closes #1039, #1041, #1043, #1045, #1048, #1050, #1052, #1056

commit.message:
Use the prebuilt H graph during HDBSCAN (#250)

# HDBSCAN Hierarchy Refactor

The previous `cluster_hierarchy` flow mixed graph state and external
bookkeeping (`label_map`, `cluster_members`). That made the algorithm
harder to reason about and added avoidable synchronization overhead.

The refactor goal was to let the graph library do the graph work. We
were manually reproducing things that NetworkX already does well
(component discovery, node-attribute state, edge-driven updates). The
new version keeps behavior stable, removes manual bookkeeping, and
leaves a clear placeholder for future temporal-overlap logic.

## Summary Table

| Area | Before | After | Why This Is Better |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Cluster state | Split across `H`, `label_map`, and `cluster_members` |
Stored on `H` node attributes (`cluster_id`, `temp_cluster_id`) | One
source of truth, fewer moving parts |
| Affected-region discovery | Label lookup + member map + subgraph
component pass | Seeded `node_connected_component` from current edge
endpoints | Direct graph-native traversal, less plumbing |
| Event node extraction | Intermediate graph construction in some
iterations | Direct union of edge index levels (`from`, `to`) | Less
overhead, simpler logic |
| Noise handling | Repeated relabeling paths | Drop noise/spurious nodes
from `H`; snapshot reindex writes `-1` | Cleaner lifecycle, fewer
repeated writes |
| Per-scale snapshots | More conversion steps and unnecessary recasts |
Direct snapshot from node attributes + reindex fill | Clear intent, less
data wrangling |
| Future overlap work | No clear staging point | Explicit placeholder
where parent children will be de-overlapped | Easier next refactor |

## Some changes

1. Removed external label/member state
- Deleted `label_map` and `cluster_members` from the hierarchy builder.
- `cluster_id` now lives on graph nodes and is updated in-place.

2. Kept traversal local to changed structure
- For each scale, remove that scale's edges.
- Build seeds from edge endpoints.
- Traverse connected components from those seeds only.

3. Made temporary child ids explicit and per-scale
- `temp_cluster_id` is reset each scale and reused only within that
scale.
- It is used as a visited marker to prevent reprocessing duplicate seeds
landing in the same component.

4. Simplified singleton handling
- If a discovered singleton has no self-loop, it is removed immediately
from `H` (noise at that scale).

5. Simplified spurious-component cleanup
- Spurious components are collected and removed from `H` in bulk per
parent.
- Surviving components are either retained under parent label (single
child) or assigned new labels (split case).

6. Simplified label-history materialization
- Initial history frame is constructed directly from known initial state
(`cluster_id = 0`).
- Per-scale snapshot is taken from `nx.get_node_attributes(H,
'cluster_id')` and reindexed to all original pings with `fill_value=-1`.
- This keeps output contract stable while reflecting dropped nodes as
noise.

This refactor intentionally does not implement temporal-overlap
resolution yet.
It only creates the correct staging point and data flow for that next
step.

## Notes

This refactor removes manual cluster bookkeeping and shifts the workflow
toward built-in NetworkX operations. Instead of keeping separate
label/member objects in sync, labels are stored directly on graph nodes
and components are discovered from the edge endpoints affected at each
scale. This makes the logic shorter, easier to follow, and less
error-prone.

The main intent was to stop re-implementing graph operations in Python
when NetworkX already provides optimized, well-tested methods for them.
We now lean on those methods directly, and keep our code focused on
stop-specific decisions (spuriousness checks and label assignment).

This commit does not add temporal-overlap resolution yet. It
intentionally prepares for it by leaving a clear placeholder after child
discovery and before final split decisions.

## Validation Performed

1. Stop detection tests
- `pytest nomad/tests/test_stop_detection.py`
- Result: `50 passed, 1 xfailed` (expected xfail retained)

2. HDBSCAN demo notebook
- `examples/hdbscan_demo.ipynb` executed successfully.
- Produced a non-empty plot with sensible stop circles and barcode
output.
- Overlap in stops is present and expected at this stage.

fix(blender): add asyncio.Lock to serialize concurrent socket calls

Root cause: viewport_broadcast_loop (every 3s) + /api/preview both call
blender.call_tool() concurrently on the same TCP socket. Without a lock,
write/read operations interleave → one caller consumes the other's response
→ the other hangs for 30s (timeout) → returns 1x1 placeholder PNG.

one coroutine uses the socket at a time. Others queue without blocking
the event loop.

fix: correct npm lockfile-only flag and handle missing lockfiles in sync-biome-version hook (#2627)

The `sync-biome-version` pre-commit hook was unreliable due to two bugs:
an invalid npm flag that silently fell back to a full install, and a
condition that skipped lockfile regeneration when files were missing but
the version was already current.

## Changes

- **`npm install --lockfile-only` → `npm install --package-lock-only`**:
`--lockfile-only` is not a valid npm option — npm warns it will stop
working in the next major version and falls back to a full install
(creating `node_modules/`). `--package-lock-only` is the correct flag
that updates only `package-lock.json`.

- **Added missing-lockfile guard to both conditions**: The original
check only ran when the version in `package.json` differed from the
pre-commit `rev`. If a lockfile was deleted while the version was
already up-to-date, it would never be regenerated.

- **`demo/frontend` migrated from yarn to npm**: `demo/frontend` was
switched from yarn to npm in main. The hook now uses `npm install
--package-lock-only` and checks for `package-lock.json` (instead of
`yarn install --no-immutable` / `yarn.lock`) for that directory.
`demo/frontend/yarn.lock` has been removed and replaced with
`package-lock.json`.

```bash
# Before (martin-ui)
if ! grep -q "\"@biomejs/biome\": \"$VERSION\"" martin/martin-ui/package.json; then
  (cd martin/martin-ui && npm install --lockfile-only)
fi

# After (martin-ui)
if ! grep -q "\"@biomejs/biome\": \"$VERSION\"" martin/martin-ui/package.json || [ ! -f martin/martin-ui/package-lock.json ]; then
  (cd martin/martin-ui && npm install --package-lock-only)
fi

# Before (demo/frontend)
if ! grep -q "\"@biomejs/biome\": \"$VERSION\"" demo/frontend/package.json; then
  (cd demo/frontend && yarn install --no-immutable)
fi

# After (demo/frontend)
if ! grep -q "\"@biomejs/biome\": \"$VERSION\"" demo/frontend/package.json || [ ! -f demo/frontend/package-lock.json ]; then
  (cd demo/frontend && npm install --package-lock-only)
fi
```

- **Lockfiles removed and regenerated** using the corrected commands to
produce a clean baseline.

automatically provides retry, request ID, user-agent telemetry, distributed
tracing, logging, and BearerTokenAuthenticationPolicy with built-in token
caching — matching the standard pattern used by all Azure SDK .NET clients.

commit.message:
feat(research): meal response analysis & insulin model improvement (EXP-1921–1928)

8 experiments investigating why demand scale is 2.2× higher during meals:
- EXP-1921: Supply error is 93% of meal error (carb absorption model is the problem)
- EXP-1922: Insulin acts 30% faster than modeled (52min vs 75min to effect)
- EXP-1923: 8.14× insulin stacking in meal windows, 1.36× loop amplification
- EXP-1924: Almost no pre-bolusing (1%), late boluses 1.26× worse error
- EXP-1925: Loop corrections 0.72U/meal average (3 patients get 1.6-2.9U)
- EXP-1926: Gradient demand model +21.3% improvement, wins 9/11
- EXP-1927: Temporal CV validates gradient model +37.4% over profile (5/11 wins)
- EXP-1928: Naive TIR projection fails (-18.6%) — need context-aware correction

Key reversal: meal-time demand miscalibration is actually SUPPLY overestimation.
Carb absorption model is the #1 improvement target, not insulin activity curve.
